title: Transport
shortSummary: Navigate buses, trains, taxis, and flights. Buy tickets, understand schedules, and get around in Spanish-speaking cities.
culturalNote: Public transport in major Spanish-speaking cities is generally excellent and affordable. Madrid and Barcelona have extensive metro systems. In Latin American cities, 'colectivos' (shared minibuses) are common and follow set routes marked by numbers. Taxis in many countries don't use metres — agree on a price beforehand. Ride-sharing apps like Uber operate in many Latin American cities but may face legal grey areas.

keyPhrases.pronunciation: DOHN-deh ehs-TAH lah pah-RAH-dah deh ow-toh-BOOS

keyPhrases.spanish: Un billete de ida y vuelta, por favor
keyPhrases.pronunciation: oon bee-YEH-teh deh EE-dah ee BWEL-tah

keyPhrases.english: What time does the train leave?
keyPhrases.pronunciation: ah keh OH-rah SAH-leh ehl trehn

keyPhrases.english: How long does it take to get there?
I want to go to the airport
keyPhrases.context: Telling a taxi driver your destination

keyPhrases.english: Does this bus go to the centre?
Where do I need to get off?

keyPhrases.context: Telling the driver you want to get off

keyPhrases.english: Is there a metro in this city?
I need a taxi to the hotel

grammar.explanation: 'Ir' is completely irregular: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van. It's one of the most used verbs in Spanish. Use 'ir a' + place for destination, and 'ir en' + transport for the method.

examples.en: I go to work by metro
Are you going by bus or taxi?

grammar.commonMistake: Saying 'yo vo' or 'yo io' instead of 'yo voy' — 'ir' is completely irregular.

grammar.explanation: To express obligation, use 'tener que' + infinitive: 'tengo que ir' (I have to go). This is the most common way to say 'must' or 'have to' in everyday Spanish.

examples.es: Tengo que bajar en la próxima
examples.en: I have to get off at the next one
You have to buy a ticket

grammar.commonMistake: Using 'tener de' instead of 'tener que' — always use 'que' before the infinitive.

dialogue.setup: A traveller asks for help at a train station in Madrid.

lines.speaker: Viajero
lines.spanish: Buenos días. Quiero ir a Barcelona.
lines.english: Good morning. I want to go to Barcelona.

lines.speaker: Viajero
lines.spanish: Ida y vuelta, por favor. ¿Cuánto cuesta?
lines.english: Return, please. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Taquillera
lines.spanish: Son noventa y cinco euros. El tren sale a las diez.
lines.english: It's 95 euros. The train leaves at ten.

lines.speaker: Taquillera
lines.spanish: El AVE tarda dos horas y media.
lines.english: The AVE (high-speed train) takes two and a half hours.

lines.speaker: Viajero
lines.english: Perfect. Which platform does it leave from?

lines.speaker: Taquillera
lines.spanish: Andén número cuatro. Tiene que estar quince minutos antes.
lines.english: Platform four. You need to be there fifteen minutes early.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I go to work by bus'?
What does 'ida y vuelta' mean?
quiz.explanation: 'Ida' = going, 'vuelta' = return. Together they mean a return ticket.

quiz.question: _____ que comprar un billete. (I have to buy a ticket)
quiz.explanation: 'Tener que' + infinitive = to have to. 'Tengo que comprar' = I have to buy.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'What time does the train leave?'
quiz.explanation: 'Salir' = to leave/depart. 'Sale' is the él/ella form.

quiz.question: Arrange: autobús / al / va / este / centro
